_meta.role: Consolidated pricing reference — every credit figure and value band scattered across the campaign files, in one place.
sources: campaign_state.md (Price book; Dungeon-goods floor; Auction house market; Guild fees)
dungeon_economy_v1.md (the tier x grade scaling model; boss-drop bands)
essence_aspects_v1.md (rarity-grade scale; core value in ME; sample core prices)
_meta.current_to: Session 30 in progress; figures are stable references the world quotes. Rarity model reworked S30 — four-rarity scarcity scale; see rarity_grades.
_meta.note: Many values are deliberately ranges or qualitative bands, not fixed numbers — the world prices in analog 'believed-price' (assayers read a value, they don't display one). Town-counter figures are a Tin/low-tier BASELINE that scales UP by tier (see tier_grade_pricing_model).

principles.town_counter_is_a_baseline: The Argent buy-book figures are what the town counter sees — overwhelmingly Tin/Copper goods. They are a low-tier baseline, not a flat grade price; higher-tier drops scale up by the multiplier model.
principles.assayed_vs_unassayed: A licensed-assayed good fetches near the floor confidently; an identical UNassayed good fetches ~a third, grudgingly. Unassayed unknowns fetch ~nothing.
principles.aspect_premium: Aspected goods (earth, fire, beast, etc.) carry a premium over unaspected.
principles.bonus_quest_rewards_never_money: Posted bonus-quest rewards pay in goods (an aspected core of choice, a crafting material, a boon), never coin — though their value reads on the same scale.
principles.for_bill: A Platinum aspected core is rarely worth selling to Bill — it is worth far more in his hands as filter feedstock / binding fuel / convert-for-mana than on the counter.
principles.frontier_markup: Materials are western, workshops and customers are eastern; frontier stock is thin, late, and marked up. Eastern buyers' agents take combat orbs and tomes.

everyday_and_town.clarion_subscription: a few cr/week, daily home delivery

services.letters: a few cr, 2-3 weeks each way
services.runner_errands: a few cr; a wagered fast run ~10 cr all-in

guild_and_assay_fees.registration: 300 cr — mandatory assay / re-assay & cultivator registration (a record)
guild_and_assay_fees.licensed_assay_fee: ~tens of cr for a guardian-core read

cartridges.note: 'my shelf, your cash' at the gunsmith; reloading kit owned

coil_gun_ammunition.deep_belt: ~500 cr bought the gunsmith at the wheel till dawn for ~100 bodkins + ~15 range darts + wedges + a dozen-odd broadheads, AND a standing retainer / first-call on his wheel
coil_gun_ammunition.cost_driver: steel is cheap; the cost is grindstone time — bodkins fast, range darts slower, broadheads the chokepoint
coil_gun_ammunition.self_build_note: S30-forward: Bill can craft his own steel dart bodies via the Crafting Table + portable hellfire; the gunsmith's standing supply is being redefined toward grindstone-finishing and first-call, not raw body supply.

crafting_and_sundry.earth_scrap: ~10 cr — a Common earth worked fragment (bought off Pearce as filter feedstock)

dungeon_goods_town_counter_floor._source: campaign_state.md (Argent buy book — TOWN COUNTER / Tin-low-tier baseline)
dungeon_goods_town_counter_floor._warning: These are the LOW-TIER BASELINE. A Platinum boss core is ~700-950+, NOT 140-190 — scale up by tier_grade_pricing_model.

tier_grade_pricing_model._basis: Multiplier applied to the Tin/floor baseline above; a drop's worth = tier x grade x quality x aspect x source.
tier_grade_pricing_model._note: Tier here = the SITE tier (Tin/Copper/Iron/Silver/Gold/Platinum), which rates dungeons. It is independent of the rarity grade (scarcity of the drop). A Platinum site can drop a Common core; a Tin site could in principle drop a Rare one.
tier_grade_pricing_model.worked_example: A Platinum Uncommon core = ~560-1,100 cr (x4-6 on the ~140-190 Tin Uncommon), before aspect or boss premiums.

platinum_floor_boss_core.note: Uncommon-Rare, high quality, faint fight-pattern; fire/earth/beast premium on top. A beast-boss core like the Ash-Maw: ~700-1,000 cr.

boss_drops.platinum_floor_boss_material_or_elixir: tens to low-hundreds cr (more for genuinely scarce restoratives)
boss_drops.platinum_dungeon_boss_core: low thousands cr — auction-headline, eastern-agent, sometimes one-buyer
boss_drops.platinum_dungeon_boss_aspected_material: hundreds to low thousands cr (more for maker-prized made-stock, e.g. Infernal forge-stock)

auction_house_market.assayed_cores: near floor confidently; identical unread cores at a third
auction_house_market.schedule: weekly Tuesday auctions + a thin daily direct counter
auction_house_market.note: The Clarion's printed delve-market figures are a soft third-hand echo — trust the shape, not the digits.

rarity_grades._basis: SCARCITY of a core or material — how rare the thing is, not how strong. Distinct from a dungeon's tier/grade (rates sites) and from an orb's level (1-5). Assayers grade on this scale, returning aspect + rarity grade at the depth the reader's skill allows.
rarity_grades.scarcity_not_power: Rarity answers how many exist, never how powerful. A Common core can carry a fierce aspect; a Rare one can be a dud. Power is carried by the Legendary/Mythic power-tags, the orb level, the aspect, and the believed-price — not by the rarity grade.

ladder.note: the overwhelming majority; ordinary banking fuel, floor-priced (a cracked one reads Common, damaged — at/below floor)
a real step up; a premium above floor (the gatekeeper core sits here)
genuinely scarce; specialist money. High Rare is the top of the repeatable, classifiable world — the rarest thing that still has a reference class to grade against

rarity_grades.unique: OFF the graduated scale: literally one-of-a-kind, no exact duplicate ever made. With no reference class, a licensed read returns family/category, not contents (the mechanical reason the great forge-core wouldn't close to Pearce's read). Cracking a Unique to its contents needs a far higher license (Saint Orison) or a reader native to its grammar (Bill's Deep Inspection, formerly Forceread).
rarity_grades.blueprint_rule: A PLAIN Unique CAN be reverse-engineered/blueprinted. The instant a second exact copy exists it stops being Unique: the original and the copy both reclassify onto the standard track, entering at Rare and degrading toward Uncommon, then Common, as the make is mass-produced. Making a true twin spends the uniqueness for both.
rarity_grades.legendary_and_mythic: POWER-TAGS, applied ONLY to Unique items — not rungs on the ladder. They mark a Unique too perfect to reduce to a repeatable process: it CANNOT be blueprinted into true copies at all. Reverse-engineering one yields a lesser standard-track replica (Rare at best, falling from there) while the original stays Unique and tagged. Mythic is the higher, rarer mark; Legendary the merely storied.
rarity_grades.aspect: Rides alongside the grade: unaspected, or a named aspect (earth, fire, electric/storm, poison, beast, ghost, ...). A made/worked resonance (forge-resonant, 'made stock') is noted as such.
rarity_grades.orbs_are_separate: Skill orbs keep their LEVEL (1-5) as their grade — the rarity scale is for cores and materials, not orbs.
rarity_grades._retired_epic_note: Epic is RETIRED as a grade in the S30 rework. Any 'Epic' tag surviving in older session files is stale — read it as High Rare, or as a Unique power-tag, per context. Value bands once filed under Epic/Legendary/Mythic now attach to High Rare and to Unique (believed-price / one-buyer).

core_value_in_ME._source: essence_aspects_v1.md (mana-equivalent — fuel value, not sale price)
core_value_in_ME._note: ME = mana-equivalent; what a core is worth as fuel, distinct from its cr sale price. Pool max reference: 210.

qualitative_value_bands._note: Where the catalogs give a feel rather than a figure, they use these bands. Rough ladder, lowest to highest.

sample_anchors.Keen Edge orb: a few hundred cr at the cage, more in private sale
sample_anchors.restricted_orbs: rarely sold openly; high private price (e.g. Phase, Aura of Suppression)
sample_anchors.high_value_orbs: Life/heal-type and last-resort orbs — medics and the Church bid; expensive
